title: Webbing a drop-in chair seat
subtitle: Interlace taut, staple at both ends, test the bounce
excerpt: How to fit a new jute webbing base to a drop-in chair seat frame: removing the old webbing, calculating the webbing layout, stretching and stapling the first half, interlacing the second half, and testing the tension before re-covering.
sourceNotes: Traditional upholstery practice. City & Guilds Upholstery Level 2 syllabus. Spratt's Practical Upholstery (1929) and David James A Complete Course in Upholstery (2014). Webbing tension and interlacing technique unchanged from 19th century workshop practice.

recipeTools.notes: If using tacks rather than staples on a traditional frame.

aliases: how to re-web a chair seat

glossaryTerms.definition: A 50 mm woven or rubber strip stretched across a chair frame as the seat-base support layer. Jute (black-and-white) for traditional sprung seats; Pirelli rubber for modern unsprung seats.

content.text: The
webbing

is the foundation of a drop-in seat. All the padding, foam, and cover material rests on it. Webbing that sags collapses the whole seat; webbing stretched tight and interlaced correctly supports years of use without movement.
Stripping the old webbing
Step 1, remove the old webbing.

Use the staple remover to lift each staple or tack from the frame. Work from the underside of the frame. Remove all old webbing, staples, and tacks completely; raised staple heads prevent the new webbing sitting flat on the frame.
Planning the layout
Step 2, plan the webbing rows.

Webbing is interlaced over-under at 90 degrees. A standard drop-in seat takes 3 to 5 runs front-to-back and 3 to 5 runs side-to-side, depending on the frame size. Space the rows evenly with no more than a half-webbing gap between them; tight spacing means more support. Mark the starting positions on the frame with a pencil.
Fitting the front-to-back rows
Step 3, staple the first end.

Cut a length of webbing about 100 mm longer than the frame depth. Fold 25 mm of the webbing back on itself at one end to double the thickness at the staple point; this prevents the staple tearing through. Position the folded end on the back rail of the frame, fold-side down, and drive three staples across the webbing width.
Step 4, stretch and staple the front end.

Hook the webbing stretcher over the front rail and lever the webbing taut. The stretcher creates tension beyond what is possible by hand; this is essential, since hand-stretched webbing sags within weeks under the sitter's weight. Maintaining the tension with the stretcher in place, drive three staples across the webbing into the front rail. Cut the webbing 25 mm beyond the staples and fold the tail back, stapling through the fold.
Step 5, complete all front-to-back rows.
Repeat for each front-to-back row, spacing them evenly across the frame.
Interlacing the side-to-side rows
Step 6, weave the side-to-side rows.

Starting from one side rail, weave each side-to-side strip over and under the front-to-back rows in an alternating pattern. The first strip passes over the first front-to-back row, under the second, over the third; the second strip reverses the pattern. This interlacing locks the two directions together and prevents the webbing sliding.
Step 7, stretch and staple the side rows.
Stretch and staple the side-to-side rows in the same way as the front-to-back rows.
Testing

Press the centre of the webbing with both hands. It should feel firm and springy with no slack. An area that gives without resistance was not stretched enough or has a loose staple. Re-stretch and re-staple before proceeding to the cover.
Common mistakes
Webbing sags after a few months of use.
The webbing was hand-tensioned without the stretcher. Re-strip and re-web using the stretcher throughout.
Staples pulling through the webbing at one end.
The fold-back was not done, or only one or two staples were used per end. Three staples into the folded double thickness are the minimum for a secure fixing.
